Hey, it's Andre and Sean. We are in week five, believe it or not, of the Momentum Builder Workshop, Lean Edition. It seems like it was only yesterday. We were just getting started. I'm sure for all of you, it feels like you've been going nonstop. These have been doing an awful lot of work. So today, we're going to talk about promotional emails. And the way we do promotional emails is very different. The way we think about promotional emails is very different. And what makes our approach very different is that we really want people to see promotional emails when they're ready and when they've given us explicit permission. Instead of just hammering the list over and over, here's a new thing, here's another new thing, we want someone to take the step and raise his or her hand and say, that thing that you've been describing all along is interesting to me. I would like to know more. Or I don't want to know more by not deciding to move forward. So because that's so different and because that's really, I think if there's any person in the world who is associated with that method, it's Andre. He's really the godfather of this approach. So I'm going to turn it over to Andre and put all the pressure on him. So why a bridge email? Why explicit permission? Like walk us through your thinking on that. Yeah, I've got nothing further to say. You've said it all. Perfect. Okay, then we're moving on. Yeah, I mean, this method is certainly after three emails can seem very weird. Like, why the hell are you doing this? Somebody's just opted in three emails ago, while we're asking him to raise their hand again. It's almost, when you guys see us doing this, you've been on an email list for, you know, weeks, months, sometimes years. So when we send out a bridge email, an email asking people to raise their hands, I guess it's less jarring because we're asking for permission in that moment. But the concept is still the same. There's a few layers to this of which we've tried our best to externalize it in a way that's clear in the lesson, even with a little doodle, a little diagram of a model that we think of, which is kind of why we do this. So, you know, everybody have raised, you know, they've clicked an ad, they've seen a message. It's been interesting enough for them to click. They've landed on your landing page, they've met it through all the way to the end, and they've opted in. So, you know, that's a lot of things that they've done. And then they've gone through three emails. Well, at any point on that journey is somebody, you know, could have got that far because, you know, because they slightly passive, they were curious, perhaps, and they just wanted to see where this thread left, you know, led. So, this is why this is really important, you know, in any moment in time, somebody is going to be, this is going to be perfect for somebody, and it's not going to be perfect for other people. And circumstances change, you know, environmental stuff change, somebody can lose a job yesterday. So, it's all these things that are outside of our control. So, this bridge email is a mechanism to call out people by explicitly getting them to raise their hand. And it's also an opportunity to really draw attention to the fact that this is a promotion. It's about this thing. It's about this thing. You can then reflect back of what it is. And at some point in that, you know, in the dialogue or in that, you know, expression, this is going to feel like something that somebody either wants to be a part of and hear more about or not. So, the way that we kind of visualize this is the image you'll see, I don't know, maybe about a half of the way down, one third of the way down the page of what the little people and the three question marks. And just the way that we've, we mentally frame this is it's, it's, it's the jobs to be done, it's pains and gains. And each one of those things can be thought of on a scale of zero to 10 or one to 10. It's probably not going to be a one or a zero because they've been through many steps to get to this point. But it's a huge, you know, it's not a linear in the sense that a father between a father and a six and a six and a seven, there's, you know, that's a big shift. A father is not anywhere close to somebody that's, that's a seven or an eight. So, I think that's, that's kind of how we think about this. And we want to, we want to try and get people to raise their hands that are hard in all three of those things, because ultimately, they're going to be, you know, if you follow the whole jobs to be done thing, it's they essentially going to be hiring our product or your product to help them, you know, solve a problem. So move towards something and away from something else. So they're going to, you know, some, some, some pain that they, that they're experiencing in their life that they don't want to have anymore, or they want to gain something, move closer to something. And you, you want to have all of those things fairly hard. So I think that's, that's the main obvious reason why we do this. And there's a few other layers to this, but, you know, perhaps we can, we can get the end of it. What do you think, Sean? Yeah, and I think there's a, there are a couple of things I think are really fascinating about this. One is that it's, it's, it's natural. It's actually how people communicate in general. I mean, if you're having a conversation with somebody over the course of a, some period of time, if you can imagine from the Facebook ad through the landing page and three email, like, that's a conversation. You're creating a world for somebody to occupy, or sort of, you're explaining how you think about something. But it's, it would be a very natural way to be looking at somebody's body language, to be reacting to the questions that they ask, to understanding, okay, are they interested in this? Are they, are they checking their phone? Are they, like, what's going on here? And we would want some signal. We wouldn't ask for an explicit signal. We might, but we would want, we would want some signal to know that this was the right time to talk about what could be next. We wouldn't just tell everybody. So from that perspective, this just makes so much more sense. It's one of those things that once you hear it, it makes, from my perspective, it makes perfect sense. Like, of course, you would do it this way. You wouldn't keep going without knowing the other person is, is leaning forward, lean towards you, like they're interested. And then there's a tactical, that functional thing that I think is interesting about this. And I don't know if I can explain this well. So I'll just, I'll think out loud for a second. That when, if we imagine the ad through landing page through relationship building emails through promotional emails is one thing. And we're going, and then we, we try to understand what's happening when we get results. So let's just say for the sake of argument that we, for every hundred people who you click on an ad, opt in, read our emails that by, for every hundred of those people, five of them buy whatever it is that we're, we have in our promotional emails. That's a signal. But it's, but it's, it's actually it's a, it's a, it's a fairly blunt signal because we don't know based on that is, is the offer. Could the offer be better or worse, or is the audience not really that engaged? And when we introduce this step in between, where there's this, this bridge email, we get this a little extra granularity that's really valuable is what it shows us is that, okay, let's say that same hundred people, instead of a hundred people seeing everything and five becoming customers, what if we saw that of the hundred people who come through, only 10 of them actually click on the thing to say, I want to learn more and of those 10, five end up buying. Well, that's, that's really different. Because now we know that it's, it's not that the offer is really strong that 50% conversion rate for people who see the offer at that point, we don't need to change how we're expressing our offer. We know that's excellent. But where we might be able to affect the system more is to get a different result is to get, is before the offer to build, maybe we need to focus on creating more attention or changing our targetting or whatever it is. But we know where to focus our attention. When we, when it's just the whole thing, we don't know if it's the audience. We don't know if it's the attention. We don't know if it's the offer itself. Sometimes we get everything right, but the offer just doesn't land. And we just don't, we don't describe it well. But we don't know that because we have this blunt instrument of measurement, which is we see the whole thing. But when we put that, that bridge email in there, let's, let's look at it a different way. Let's assume that a hundred people get through to the bridge email and of those hundred 70 click. Well, now we know we've really built anticipation, but of those 70, only five purchase, that's a very different. Now that I would look at that and think, okay, well, we've done our job really well. We find the right audience. We've really built up anticipation. They want to know more. But the more that we showed them, wasn't very interesting. And I would focus my attention then on making the offer better and, and, and sort of changing that. So I like this, I like this other layer of granularity, but I think more than anything, it just feels like being good humans. If you just like, I want to be treated this way. And I want someone to say to me, are you interested in more instead of just assuming that just because I've read things so far that now is the right time for me. I just, it feels everything about this feels like a better, a better way to do it. Yeah, I mean, there's, there's a reality that'll, that'll play out. And it'll play us out like this every single time. It's, it's never not going to play out like this. It'll be, it'll be nuanced. But it'll, it'll go something like this where when you're looking at your open, your, your open rates from email one, two, three, and bear in mind, this is, these are cold, cold leads that have, that have, that have come in to your world. They don't know you from, from Adam. They've, they've raised their hand for email number one, which is why email number one will most likely have the highest open rate of the three. And then it'll, it'll kind of taper down from that. But I think it's safe to say that with, with cold, with cold leads, if you're getting a 30% open rate, and again, this is, this is just very broad. That's probably pretty good. But, you know, 30% of, of the people that are joining that list are actually, you know, engagements with, with those three emails is, you know, think about that for a second, right? So these are brand new people. They've just raised their hand and still 70% of them haven't opened the email or clicked a link or done anything. That's a signal of engagement. I am, and again, you know, stats aren't, aren't foolproof. There's, you know, there's, there's many factors, which is why you'll never see 100%. But here's what happens. So when you present the, the bridge email, and you know, you're still going to get the 30%, let's say, sometimes it'll be a bit higher. But what will happen is from that segment of people that are clicking and raising your hand. So let's say the, in this scenario, your bridge email is number four, which means the, the, the first email for the promotion will be five. So five, six, seven. Well, the open rates of that segment, it's going to go from 30% to 80% or 70%. It'll be, it'll be, it'll be a lot higher. It'll be double. It'll be, it'll be noticeably higher because something's happened where you've, we've, we've done something and you've, you've extracted all the people that are, that want to see this thing play out, that have raised the hand, that have been explicit. They've, there's a level of commitment. It's, it's a proper pain they have or again, they want to get a job they want to do. And, and, and by them being explicit about clicking a link, they're, they're like radars suddenly being, being turned on to the thing that's, that's going to happen. There's a level of excitement and that's, you know, that, that's, that's shown out in the stats, even if the stats aren't accurate. It's in terms of seeing it in the context inside of your, your ESP, ESP dashboard, it's significantly higher. But again, like as Sean said just now that this is a great way to, to see signals happening in real time. And then if there's no sales, it's like, ah, okay. You know, you're going to get more data that you can then make informed decisions about, you know, maybe the problem is, is the, are the first three emails or, or, or the bridge email or the promotion or the offer. There's, there's all these, there's all these pieces that we try to figure out before we have a system that's, that's dialed in and working predictably every single time. And without the bridge email, it's a very different beast. You don't know anything other than to earn any sales or not. And even then that's, that's a terrible indicator of what's working or what's, or what's not working. If you get two sales out of 100 people, it still tells you very little. You don't, you don't know much because the next day it might be five sales and the next day might be zero sales. So you really have very little information that you're working with other than just, you know, having your finger in the end. So I think that's, it's worth highlighting that, that piece. There are a couple of other things that just came to mind to you that I think are important to mention. One is this is going to feel like some people as that they're, they're losing something, right? There's going to, I can imagine someone listening to this, they're reading that, that, that lesson and thinking, well, wait a minute, I, you know, I've done the work here. I got the ad, they went to the landing page, I got it, I've done the relationship building stuff like that. Now's the time. Like why would I ever put up a barrier? But the reality is the people who are not six, seven, eight, you know, to 10, really seven, eight, nine, 10s, those people aren't going to move forward to the promotion anyway. You're not losing anybody by doing this. There's, there's no, if you look at this, this strategy from 10,000 feet, it's all upside because the people who are not going to move forward at this time feel better because, and they haven't seen your offer yet. You haven't shown them something that they're not interested in. They like how you've treated them. If you've moved them over to your newsletter or whatever, you didn't show them a promotion. They have, they don't even know there's a promotion there yet. So it's all upside. Those people were not going to convert anyway. So you didn't lose them. Now, if you're, you know, I'm sure there are some exceptions, like super high pressure, but that's, that's not what we're talking about here. And that's not what you want to try to do. It's really, really hard work to persuade somebody to do something, to really engineer the mousetrap that makes people who don't want to buy, to buy. Yes, you can do it. In our opinion, no, you shouldn't do it. But there's no loss here. And I think there's another thing I hadn't actually thought about until just now, when you were saying something, Andres, I'll give you credit for this, for me thinking about this, because we assume that people go through our, our emails and other things, the way that we structure them. So we assume just unconsciously, someone clicked on an ad and they went to a landing page, and then they opted in. We know they have to do that. It's active. Those two things are active. But once email starts showing up, we think to ourselves, okay, there are three relationship building emails. So one goes out on day one, and the person reads it, and then they're excited, then they get the second email, they read it, they're even more excited. And the third one comes, and they're really excited. And now when they get the bridge email, like this, like it's all that excitement is right there. It's day four, or why would we lose all that excitement by putting up a barrier? But the truth is, somebody might read, might opt in and read the first email, sure. But the second email might come in, and it might come in when they're doing something else. And they might think, well, I'll read that tomorrow. And then the third email comes in the next day, and they still haven't read that. But maybe they're still interested, but they're not, they're not engaged. So now imagine emails, four, five, six, are all promotional, then they see the subject line, you know, offer expires, and we've all done it, we've all seen this, like offer expires, and they're like, what the hell, like then they jump ahead, I'm gonna mean offer expires, they short circuit your entire process, because you've, you've, you've given them an opportunity to go way ahead. Instead, when we put this bridge email in, what we're saying is, you're only going to see the promotion at the, when you've caught up, they have to read the second email, or they could, sure, they could jump ahead to the third, but they're, they're, they're never going to go deep into promotional emails without first reading the email, that's the bridge email, where they can click on the thing that says, yes, I'm interested. That's what then triggers things that follow. And if it takes them a week to read your emails, then they don't see the promotion until they're actually ready, which is perfect, rather than have three promotional emails sitting in their inbox that they can just kind of skim through very quickly. So introducing this little bit of friction actually makes the process much better from the prospect's perspective and from your perspective, because you're, you're focusing their attention when they have told you. And we know this is true, because when we do, you know, anytime we do a time bound open and close promotion, invariably, we get people who reach out to us after like, oh, I'm really sorry, I didn't know about it. I hadn't read that email, I was catching up with my emails, or I, you know, I read the email before it was incredible, and I was doing some, you know, doing some work related to that, I didn't even know there was a thing. Right. So that happens all the time. And this is just a way to recognize that you can make the timing, you can always make the timing work for your prospects, which in turn makes the timing work for you. I mean, everybody wins by doing this. There is, there, it's easy to imagine downside, but the downside to be very clear is imagined. There is no downside with this approach. It is that extra, the extra friction over the longer, longer term pays dividends, and there's, there just, it doesn't come at a cost. What looks like a cost is a short term, it's a short term misperception that those people might have become customers in short out of every hundred people, maybe one or two, for whatever reason, might have become a customer. But the gain you get from that is far outweighed by the gain that you get by recognizing that sending people promotions when they've raised their hand and said they're ready is always going to create a better experience. There's just, there's no, there's no exception to that from my perspective. All right, we could talk about the bridge emails for it. It's one of those things that when you get it, and it's not to be clear, it's not, the bridge email is the embodiment of a much deeper idea, which is to, to interact with people in the way that they want to be interacted with when they're most interested. So the bridge email is the embodiment of it. The principle is to engage with your audience in ways that make sense for them, where they have an active role. And that's, that's critical, that we can't overstate the importance of that. All right, Andre, where do you want to go next? What do you think is important in the week five that we should draw some attention to? Yeah, I guess it's worth mentioning that these bridge emails and the promotions that happen on the back end of the bridge email, we haven't shared any templates because guess what? There are no templates. If you go to our email archive for every single promotion that we sent out in 2020, so last year you can access that from our website. Just go to the, go to the blog and there's an end, there's a link there and it'll take you to the, to the email minus archive page. And you can see all the promo emails there. And you'll notice something that not one promotion looks the same, smells the same, quacks the same. They're all, they're all different and unique in the own way. The only constant is there's a promotion. The bridge email was there's, there's a promotion, if you're interested, raise your hand. If not, don't click the link. And you know, most, I mean, that's, that's, that's going to someone that's, that's not interested in reads that, you know, there's, there's a level of respect that you've just given them like, like Sean mentioned early on, and, and they remember that some of them will click the link because, you know, some people are just conditioned to click links and they don't always read carefully and they don't want to be, you know, they don't want to miss out on something, which is why on the back end, at the very top of every single promotional email, and we haven't mentioned this in the lesson because we didn't want the lesson to be overly bloated. So I'll just mention it here and then we can move on. Is we have a little blurb at the top of every single promo email that if you want to remove yourself from this, this promotion, not the entire list, just this promotion, just click this link and we'll, and we'll meet you. So that, that allows people to, to really like think, ah, this is, this is different. I'm in the right place because nobody else is doing, I've, I've never seen this like this. And, you know, it's been very respectful of people's attention and time. And, you know, every single time at the end of any promotion we do, because there's a tag in the system that we create that if this tag exists, which means someone's clicked, clicked that link to be muted, they, they exclude it from any, any, any promo emails. It's, it's always very low. There's like a handful, that's it. It's because of the way this whole thing is, has been set up. And yeah, so it's worth drawing, drawing attention to that. What else? So there's a fairly long email that we've, we've given as an example of a bridge email. We could have included five, five examples, but I think that's missing the point. This isn't about sharing your five examples. You can go, you know, you receive our emails, go and find previous ones if you want, but you don't need to overthink this. The email that we shared with you is, is fairly long, but we wanted to do that because you can see that there's, there's a bit of a story going on. And, and it builds up, it builds up what's about to happen behind the click. And by the time somebody gets that, to that link to, to opt in, well, to, you know, to raise the hand or, or, or not to, it's very clear that this thing is, is on. So, you know, that's, that they want to be, be a part of this thing. And there's a heart and level of, of anticipation and excitement about it. And that's a magical thing because as soon as you start sending email number one of the promo, so in this case, it'll be email number five, it's, you, you know, that people are, they anticipating these, these, these emails of this promotion. And that's a magical feeling, you know, to, to know that people have raised their hand to receive this promotion because it's in their best interest. So yeah, I just wanted to say that. What else? Yeah, there's something, I think switching gears a little bit to the promotional emails themselves. This is one of the hardest things to describe to somebody is, and I'm sure this is everybody's question. And we've really talked about what not to do in the module a lot, like some mindset things, not to do, not to apologize, not to switch tone, not to, you know, all of a sudden turning to a used car salesman, like, oh, I've paid my dues, dammit. Now it's time to sell, sell, sell. We don't write, but there's also the question like, great, what, what do we do? And the way to think about what to do, and this is how we talk about, and this is how we write promotions, which is by the time you get to the promotion, what you're about to reveal should be the logical next step. The person should be expecting it. They should want it. And you think about building a world for somebody, you know, you've built this world, you've created, first of all, you know that they have certain needs, desires, jobs to be done, whatever it is, they have, there's something that they want. And you see structured something in a way that is attractive, knowing that they have this desire, or they want to solve this problem, or whatever it is. And then you've framed the world that they're about to enter a certain way, where maybe you've taken what seems like it's very like Jonathan Baker is a great example. He's taken something that from the outside seems like it's incredibly complicated, but the frame that he has created simplifies it. He says, you know, and he's not overtly saying this, but it's really like what you think is a thousand things really is just three things. And from his experience, there's three ways to think about going faster on a bicycle. And so that frame, you've now, you've now narrowed that person's world to say, okay, this is how to think about it. And then when they're thinking about it that way, if you're going through your landing page, and you've sort of built this world, that's a representation of your experience, maybe some stories, maybe some results that other people you know that you've worked with have have had whatever it is, you've taken them through that you're beginning to take them through that world. And when you go get into the emails, you're continuing to do that, you're continuing to focus on that relationship that you're building around this topic, that here, here's what's important. You know, now that we both see the you're not saying this explicitly, but what's really happening is you're saying, now that we both see the world the same way, right now that we both understand that if you want to go faster on a bicycle, you either need to reduce weight, reduce drag, or generate more power, right? Now that we both understand that, then then we're going to kind of go through what's next. And it may still be, maybe Jonathan's email, his first email may talk about like what, how, how, how reducing weight, how that affects going faster, right? So he's still, he's still talking about the stuff and in building that world. But at some point in this journey, the person who was sitting at the edge of his or her seat, the eight, nine, and 10s, that were in the bridging, and I was just, yes, I want to know more. What they want to know is, what the hell do I do? Right now, I like, I get it, they're nodding their head like, I get it. Like if I'm, if I'm using the Jonathan example, if, if I'm an ideal prospect, I'm nodding up and down saying, I, I, I agree. You've like, you've made your point. I, I understand that the way to go faster is to train for, you know, train intelligently for power output. And there's a way to do that that's efficient and highly effective. Excellent. How the hell am I supposed to do that? And that's the point at which it's just very natural. You know, you're thinking in the back of your mind, well, I'm glad you asked, because I've spent a lot of time thinking about this. And here's how, here's what I've created to answer that question. And you can even externalize that. And that can even be part of, part of the process somewhere to engage with the reader actively and say, and you could do this in the bridge email, or you could do it in the first promotional email, but to recognize, you know, I like to think about bringing everything together. It's almost like they're, and I think, Andre, you've drawn this in ways, but I'm probably imagining a drawing that you did. But it's a, it's like there's a point that opens, I'm seeing it horizontally, but it's like there's a point that opens up and then there's a long journey. And that journey includes the ad, the landing page, the three emails, but that journey then comes again to another point, which is let me, let me almost summarize where we are. I do this very naturally when I write, because I have to orient myself. When I'm writing, I tend to orient myself to where, where I am, because I know I'm going somewhere. So to do that in this process is really to say, like, let's, and you don't need to like be over and let me restate what we got. It's more like a, you're letting them, you're reminding the audience that this is sort of where we are, right? We get it. And I would almost say that's something that I'm not going to write copied in my head here, thinking out loud, but it would be something that effectively like for Jonathan's example, you know, before he transitions to the author, I would say something like, you know, I, you know, at this point, I, I know, I understand what you're thinking, right? So you, you, you get it to go faster on a bicycle. Like, if we, if we want to reduce weight, we can spend a lot of money and we're not going to get a lot of result. We get that. And if we, if we can reduce drag, there are ways we can do that. We can rent the wind tunnels, we do all these things, but we're not really going to get a lot of impact. The, the place to focus our attention is on generating more power. And there are ways to do that effectively. And I, I, my guess is that right now you're, you're wondering to yourself, great, Jonathan, I agree with you 100%. Now, what the hell am I supposed to do? Or how do I actually do that? And that's the transition, you know, and, you know, I, and Jonathan might say something like, you know, I've been thinking about that question for the last 30 years. And, and I, and my answer to that question is, and that's when he starts, he makes the transition in the way I've answered that question is to recognize that the, that the train that they're, or to have created a training program that has a few characteristics that are really important. Then you map those characteristics to the needs of the audience and the desires you've been talking about. This is a way things can go really wrong. The offer is revealed, but the offer has no relationship to the world that was just built. So Jonathan could do something where he, he reveals an offer, but the offer isn't about exactly the thing that he described as the problem. And we sense that even if, even if the offer is really good, if it doesn't match what he described, if he does, if it doesn't match the, the tension that he's created perfectly, then it doesn't seem like the logical next step. It seems like somebody just did a bunch of work to get my attention to then show me an offer. The offer has to be the thing that solves the issues or creates the outcomes that everything else was about. At this point, it's just, it should be so natural that it's that you're not selling, you're not persuading, you're just describing at this point, you've reached this point where you, you're really saying, you know, if you want to go faster on a bicycle and you understand that to do that, you need a training program that's all about power output. And you don't have six hours a day to do it. You want to be able to accomplish that efficiently in an hour or less a day. I've spent the last 30 years thinking about this question. And because of that, I've created a program that does, and it, the thing the program does is exactly what he has described as being what they want earlier. And then you transition to, and here's, here's how it works. Like that's, that's the transition to promotion. It's not, you know, it's not because it's a promotion, it's bye, bye, bye. It's not, you know, now it's time we're switching gears and today and today only it's 50%. It's not what a promotion is. The promotion is the matching of the prospect's desire on their behalf to the thing that you've created and showing them how this thing that you've created lines up with exactly what they want. So when you say, I know you will implicitly, I know this challenge that we've been talking about is X. Here's how I have, here's how I have created something that solves for X. And you have to be that explicit. And because of that, it's, it doesn't feel like a promotion. It feels just like this is what you would do next. If someone is standing next to you saying, I really wish I could solve whatever. I'll use Jonathan again, it's just a convenient example of Jonathan is talking to a competitive cyclist and that person saying, I just wish somebody could tell me what to do so that I can go faster on a bicycle without spending a ton of money for just tiny little gains. And Jonathan would just turn to him and say, I've spent a lot of time thinking about this. Here's what, like, here's how I've thought about it. Here's how I do it. And here's why I do it this way. That's three emails. That's absolutely three emails. Easy to just explain to another person why the thing that you've created makes perfect sense for them if they've made it this far in the journey. And this is, you know, Andre, we mentioned, Andre, I mentioned this before this call, we've got a little surprise for you for next week. But the surprise is going to be, we'll give you a little hint about it. The surprise is going to be related to the fact that now that you've made it all the way here, and you're at the point at which that you're writing the promotion, you're going to realize that you need to work backward from the promotion all the way back through the beginning of what you're doing to make it more seamless. But you can't do it, you can't do it in reverse order. You have to do it in the order that you did it. So now that you know the world you've created for them, your offer needs to fit perfectly into that world. And if it doesn't, you either need to change the offer or you need to change the world that you built. Because when it's one long conversation, it works. It's not disjointed. It is, it's a perfect progression. I'm all excited now. I want to write something. All right, your turn. What are you? Yeah, let me just end up by saying, you know, this one last riff, which is, in our world, it's really important that a promotion leaves people better off, even if they don't make a purchase. So there's value inside of the message, the emails, the journey we are presenting somebody, that even if they don't bar because, you know, the reality is, even with you doing a bridge email and segmenting people out and you're getting 80% open rates, most people still are not going to buy. If you get 20% of people buying, then that's great. That's, you know, there's a, there's still 80% that just in that moment, haven't purchased for whatever reason. But you want to leave them better off. You want to leave them so next time around or later on, then they're all in. So yeah, so when you read any of our promotions, there's, that's a theme that you'll see that there is value inherent in every single email. One of the things we've included in this lesson, so maybe two thirds of the way down as a look here, is both of us are visual, very visual thinkers, I'm visual to a flaw, and I almost have to draw, draw imagery, imagery before I can describe it to myself, certainly to somebody else. So I've included a little rough drawing, which essentially is, is the, is the journey of most stories. And so you can use this as a, as a mental model to, to map to your entire campaign. So from add all the way through to, to making that offer, this, this will, will work how you want to, you know, fit everything in is completely up to you. But, you know, whether it's one month long promotion or a three day, three email promotion, this can still work. So it's a nice mental model just to look at. And, you know, on the one side is people are coming in, this is their, their ordinary world, this represents where there are now. And where there are now, there's, there's, they trying to get somewhere else, they trying to attain a certain level of expertise or skill or, or, or something that's, that matters to them, you know, this, this job to be done, the pain, the gain. It's something that they don't have. And then on the other side is they, they're dreaming about this, this desired future state, or as you can see in the image, I've caught it, the, the new life. And so the bridge that, that we creating or this journey is, is a bridge from their ordinary world where they're at now to this desired future state. And you do this, as you can see in the imagery that, you know, you regard, for example, the guard doesn't have to be you, it could be somebody else. We've, we've covered this in, I think it was the, in, in the last lesson, the, the idea of a guard, but essentially it's, it's somebody that, that knows something that the person that's reading this doesn't know and they want. And it's normally in the form of a map. Again, this is, this is just, just a metaphor. And the map, you know, is, is a plan or process or system. It's a thing that can, that can take them from where they want to, to where they want to go. And along the way is you get, you give them this, this magic sword, the magic sword again, of course, it's a metaphor. It's this little thing that, although it's not going to guarantee success, it's going to amplify the ability to get to where they, where they want to go. It's not going to be without conflict. You know, it's, I think it's important to say that it's not going to be rainbows and unicorns once they, once they make a purchase. I think the people know that and understand that. But the system, this plan, this process, this magical item that, that you're going to get when you enroll or purchase whatever, it's going to stack the deck in your favor. So when you tell a story that's, that's painted, you know, that's, that's, that's set up that way. And it gives like HD clarity of what, of where somebody is, you know, again, it's, it's, I see you, I hear you, it's about empathizing of where they're at. And then at the same time, I'm saying, you know, painting that, that desired future state. So it's, it's, it's, it's an HD, high resolution clarity that, so when that offer is made, there's, there's, there's, there's very little selling that needs to be done. It's, it's really, here's the link. People know at that point, they've sold themselves, they want to go all in, or they don't. So that's how we do it. It's not the only way to do it, obviously, you only, you know, you can witness that by being on a thousand other promotions, they're all going to be very different to this. But this is how we think, based on what we've been doing for a long, long, long time, that this is the best way to, to promote anything. So that's it, I think. That's all I've got. I think I'll point everybody to one example and explain our thinking behind it. So to give it a little more context. So if you, if you look back at last year, there are a couple of lessons here that are, I think are important. So we did a fall promotion last year, and we knew that promotion was going to be your opening enrollment for three courses. So it's for autoresponder, madness, we have been the points in the traffic engine. So we knew, we knew where we were going. We knew what the promotion was going to be talking about before we did anything. And the way we did the promotion, it was 10 emails, five of those emails went to everybody. And this is the durable bit. This is, if you go and see the 2020 year review, you'll see references to the durable business, which is a little confusing because we renamed something and offered it in Black Friday, the durable business. But so that, that 10 part series, the deceit, when we, when we thought about this in advance, this is how we, this is the thinking behind it. So when you look at it, you'll understand the thinking that went into it. So we knew the, we knew that when it was time to be promoting, we were going to be promoting three courses. So what we promoted had to make sense across three different courses. So it couldn't just be about one topic. It needed to be a topic that encompassed all of them. So that's this idea of beginning with the end in mind. But we also knew, as Andre just mentioned, we wanted everybody who experienced the promotion, including the lead up to it before we mentioned anything about our products, we wanted everybody to get value the entire time so that it would be independently valuable for everyone, no matter what they, what they did, or sort of what level of engagement they chose. So I believe, if my memory is correct, we had a Friday email, we let everybody know that the following week we're doing a five part series. And that five part series was for the elements of what we talked about what a durable business is, what it means to be durable, you know, long lasting, anti fragile. And then we went through the elements of it over the course of five days. Everybody received that. And because everybody received it, there was no mention of any of our courses in it. It was not about our courses, it was about the idea. However, that idea made sense as a way to open the door to what we were going to do the following week. We were very explicit about this that there's another five days, there's part two, but part two requires your permission because we're opening enrollment for our courses at the end of part two. And we're going to talk about our courses in that. So if you raise your hand, you want the second part, you're getting the second part, but eyes wide open, we're going to be promoting some stuff. So when you when you see the whole thing, it's one cohesive whole, but it, and we know where it was leading on the 10th day, we opened enrollment. That was day one of open enrollment for our courses. So it all makes sense. But if somebody just, if somebody wasn't interested in the courses, they would get value out of those first five emails or standalone valuable. If somebody read the next five, so they had all 10, and they didn't buy a thing, there's still value in that, like we we're leaving them better off than we're finding them. And then also, it set up a way for us to talk about our courses that made sense. We created the framework, we explained how to think about business, so that our courses make perfect sense within that framework. Sounds very deliberate and like we engineered the whole thing, but for us, the way we write tends to be very emergent. So but we started with that framework and where we started was we established some guidelines. One, we don't talk about our courses for to everybody, you have to raise your hand to hear about our courses. We're going to talk about the ideas. And all of this needs to be something that somebody might like copy and paste into a document or print or refer to so that it's going to have our best thinking in it, so that if they just read all 10, and that's it, we've left them in a much, much better place, so everybody gets value. And that's really a way to that's a way to think about and don't you don't try to write like we read, we write the way that we do because of our individual ways that we write and then how we those two those individual ways come together in different ways. So don't try to like don't try to write the way we wrote it, but think about the idea that went into that where you're talking with somebody in a way that's valuable to them. And, but you're not trying to sell anything until they've said you know what I'm more interested in like, how can you help me with this problem? Well, I can but but that's where you need their permission. So just if you I think if you look at that example, from that perspective that we just shared, so you know the thinking behind it, I think that's a good example of how we think about this. All right, anything else to wrap up today, Ange, we're probably coming up to 50 or 55 minutes or so. So the risk of doing another three hour recording, we probably want to wrap up here. Anything you want to add before the before we wrap up? No, I think we've we've we've hit it all. This is our longest lesson to date in this in this workshop so far is because you know, we wanted it, we wanted to spend some time and just really unpack this idea in a way that was as precess as possible, which I think we've we've done pretty well. So yeah, I think anything more I'm just going to be waffling. So it's all in there. You don't need to know everything. It's just you just need to know enough to write your first shitty draft, which is what this this workshop is about. It's about creating that first shitty draft across an entire campaign. So yeah, it's going to be fun reading these. Yeah, I'm excited. And one last, we opened the loop earlier, we'll open it again. We'll get a little surprise for you the next week. If you made it this far, you're the first to know. And I think for now, Andre said it best, which is this workshop is about getting the work done. There's the surprise we have for you only makes sense if you've done the work. That's in those of you who are doing the work, we have crazy amounts of respect for you because we know it's hard. This is a lot to accomplish five weeks, no, we're pushing up against the edges. But when you when you see the surprise, you'll understand what you like how it all works. And because you've done the work already, it's going to it's going to have a lot of impact. If you haven't done the work, it won't be a surprise. It'll be a letdown. Do the work, finish strong, and we'll see you next week.
